sentences.text: Two and half years.
We-- basically I was actually for one year without my parents in Israel.
Because my-- as I've mentioned to you previously, my father really didn't want to be in Israel.

And I think the fact that the Israeli government took a lot of his-- basically earnings, I mean because my father brought from Poland everything, from BMW motorcycle, which he absolutely loved, to piano, to air conditioning, which they still didn't have, to refrigerator.
I mean I remember, I recall so well.
Because at the time, in 1956, when we got to Israel, the Israeli economy was still very poor.
And most of our relatives did not have all these luxuries yet.

And even, you know, my-- my mother's oldest-- eldest sister, Eva, whose husband was an architect, and he was doing quote, unquote 'very well,' they still did not have material goods, as many as we had that we brought from Poland.
But the [NON-ENGLISH]-- remember that, I still remember the word-- took almost half of what my parents brought in.
And my father was very upset, very upset about it.
Another thing is that he couldn't tolerate the very religious Jews who basically he said were running the country, yes.
Which there's a little of truth to that, but not extensive truth.
And he just wanted to get out of there as soon as he possibly could.
Meantime, I was placed again, not against my-- I really didn't want to go to nursing school.
But I was sort of told that this is where I'm going to go.
And that's it.
You know, so I was placed in Hadassah, a Tel Aviv Nursing School, after I finished ulpan.
And I started going to nursing school.
Worked about seven hours after the first month or so of being in school.
And then I went to school for six hours.
So it was a very, very difficult time.
And the thing is that I was very inquisitive.
I always wanted to be a doctor, not a nurse, and do research.
And basically, most of the doctors, if you ask them questions there, they would just say to you, you know, mind your own business type of thing.
I mean they never wanted to really explain to you anything.
And-- and I just wanted to know.
And this sort of thing did not agree with me very well.

But I was very angry at both of my parents for sort of, instead of sending me to regular high school, to finish the high school, because I had two years of Polish high school, you know, to be sent off to nursing school.
Because this is the thing that you could do after two years of high school.
You could go to that nursing school.
And they basically run my life for me, you know.
And I was starting to rebel against it, as a teenager too.
So when they had the documents prepared that they're going to go to the United States, all my friends, all my cousins would say, now they send you here to nursing school.
You don't like that.
Probably if you go to the United States, they will send you to factory to work, or something like that.
Which of course, was not true.
This was totally nonsense.
But in my mind, you know, it was like I don't want to go.
I like it here now.
And I'm going to stay here.
But I definitely am not going to stay in nursing school.
The day they left, I walked in to my nursing school counselor, and said, I'm getting out of here.
And I already had two years of nursing school, which was stupid.
Because I should have just finished, and then not do anything with it, and continue on with further education that I wanted to do.
But I was so angry at them for making me do it.
That this was my first act of like independence.
I dropped out by myself from the school.
And even though I still had like a whole year before I could go to the army or something, so I started working part time.
Because the school did not-- it was in the middle of school.
So I couldn't start any other education or anything.
So, and I felt that I have to support myself some way.
So I-- I was doing in [INAUDIBLE], basically nursing, you know, helping, you know, people-- private patients, et cetera.
I would do that.
And I-- I had been engaged already at the time to-- at the age of 17, to my gynecology professor, you know.
And I decided I'm not going to leave.
That's it.
But soon enough, the army started calling me, and I wanted to be a paratrooper.
My parents were very upset about that.
And my mother basically gave me an ultimatum.
She said, either you come here, you're under age.
And-- or I will go and get you myself and bring you to the United States.
So they basically gave me about two weeks or so to get out of there.
And that's what happened, you know.
But I had some very interesting happenings, actually, in the nursing school which I would like you-- which I would like to tell you about.
Because that's-- that was something very dear to my heart.
One of the times I was called by the Psychiatry Department if I could come and help them, because I spoke Polish and Hebrew.
And there is a young woman in the psychiatric ward who tried to commit suicide.
And she speaks only Polish.
And I went there, and I found out after a little while, I was coming to visit her several days before I got to hear what she had to say.
And one of the reasons for her trying to commit suicide was that she was only told some months ago, before the-- the trial of the suicide, that she was a Jew.
What happened was she was living in Krakow-- she was living in Krakow, and going to Krakow University.
And she was one of the foremost leaders of antisemetic outbreaks in Krakow University at the time.
Excuse me.
She was-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] basically going after all the Jews who were the students.
And she was, you know, causing some really bad riots there.
And she was caught by the police finally, and put into a prison.

And while being in prison, a person or two people, actually, from the Israeli embassy came to visit her, just to tell her that they discovered that she's a child of someone who is looking for her in Israel.
And basically, you know, they told her she's Jewish.
And she had, you know, she could do either of these two, either not to go, decide to stay away, you know, and-- or just decide to go to Israel.

So when she found out that she actually was Jewish, she saw just that this is the end of her, of her life in Poland, and especially the shame that she was discovered to be a Jew, someone who is such an antisemite, you know.
She decided that she's going to go to Israel with them.
And she came to Israel.
And she didn't want to have anything to do with that family of hers.
She was very angry at them for-- for looking for her, for discovering where she is, for uncovering who she is, for messing her life up like this.
And so she decided that she rather kills herself, you know.
And I came to talk to her [DRINKS] on several occasions.
And I told her about my childhood, and about what happened to me, et cetera.
And I tried to just help her as much as I could.
I really don't know at the end what happened to her.
But she was kept in the psychiatric ward for quite some time.
So these were not very fond memories, you know, of-- of another horrifying experience of what can happen to children of the Holocaust.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] But at the end, you know, I didn't want to be embarrassed by-- by my parents, you know.
And after several weeks of thinking about it, I-- I did go to the US Consulate in Tel Aviv, and-- and told them, you know, that I am going to go back-- you know, back to my parents to United States.
And little did I know what kind of questions they're going to be asking me.
But I was interrogated as some kind of a little Commie.
You know, it was really funny.
I remember that very vividly.
And because I was like a pioneer, you know, and-- and the thing is that you are a leader of the pioneers.
Because you happen to be a good student, you know.
So this has nothing to do with your choice of, you know, whatnot.
And I did finally-- was able to get out of Israel, within like maybe 24 hours of my-- either you-- you go to Israel or to the military, because the military was after me.
So they gave me also a limited time of getting out of Israel.
And I was really upset.
I didn't want to come to United States.
But, now I'm glad I-- I did come.
Oh, it was-- it wasn't easy.
Because I did not speak English, again.
And so going from one country to another, I had to learn a new language, and new customs.
And it was another shocking experience.
But the most wonderful experience was that my parents, unlike what people were telling me in Israel, they were delighted, you know, to-- for me to just go straight back to school.
And not only that, but they insisted on that.
So I was very happy.
But I remember my mother, and the whole family almost came to Idlewild Airport at that time to pick me up.
And my sister grew so much in one year that I could hardly-- I didn't recognize her even.
They were standing upstairs.
And I did not see anybody.
So the lady who was the stewardess was my girlfriend, and I knew all the pilots.
So one pilot was holding one of my valises.
Little-- I had very, very little packages, just little valises.
Because everybody said you're going to the United States.
You won't need anything.
You know, you will have everything in United States.
So they took all my clothing even, you know.
So I hardly had anything.
And-- and finally, you know, the pilot says, my friend, you just come with us, you know, to our hotel if they don't pick you up.
So I was-- I was, you know, walking out, when my-- all of a sudden I see my mother, as the door opened, you know, to get out already of there.
My mom and the whole family started, you know, crying and hugging me, kissing me, people that I, again, didn't know.
Because these were all the United States relatives that I did not know, my father's family.
Because in Israel, I had my mother's family mostly.
And here mostly my father's family.
[DRINKS]
So, that's what happened.
I came to United States, and immediately I went back to high school, as a matter of fact, to James Monroe High School where I finished the two years of high school that I lacked.
And it was very easy for me.
Because I already-- two years of Polish high school is much more than four years of United States high school.
So I had no problems whatsoever.
And I finished high school, and learned the language as much as I could, and went to City College, applied to City College, and was accepted to City College.
And that's what I did.
It was quite interesting.
Because why I decided on science, in some ways because I had all sorts of choices at the time.
I was upset at the beginning.
Because going to City College, you really meet-- meet very, very, very many, very bright kids.
And this was sort of like the time when I was in 1961 going to City College, it was like the Harvard of-- of the proletariat, so to speak.
And I certainly was worried that I'm not going to be able to keep up with good marks.
And they would throw me out.
That's what I-- I felt, because of my language.
My language was not so good, as I would expect.
And I was worried.
So one day I came home and I just said, you know, I think I'll major in Russian literature.
Because I spoke Russian.
And I used to be really basically studying more English in my Russian class.
I took the Russian class for that reason.
I was able to sort of-- they were studying Russian, and I was listening to the translations, you know.
And-- and I came home, and I said to my father, I think I'm going to major in Russian Literature.
He hardly spoke to me.
He was so upset.
And he said to me basically at the time, you know, if-- if I did not have the profession that I have, I would not be alive today.
And he said, today we have a great-- it's capitalism.
Everything is wonderful.
Tomorrow you don't know.
Tomorrow maybe the Communist can come.
Tomorrow maybe some other fascist can come.
You never know.
So what you need is a profession that you can support yourself.
Today you're pretty.
Tomorrow you'll be an old lady, and you will have to support yourself again.
I won't be alive for the rest of your days.
And he sort of like imprinted on me the fact that you have to study something that is with you all the time.
And what's with you all the time is science, it's engineering.
So, I guess I-- he said basically, if you could not do it, then I can understand.
Then you do whatever you can.
But if you have the brain to do it, do it.
So I stuck with it.
And I'm glad.
Very mixed up.
[LAUGHS] And-- that's-- that's very honest, truthful statement.
Because I guess I started feeling more and more that-- and I still feel that today, if there is God and there is God, there is one God for all of us.
And people just name the god different ways, and pray to the god different ways.
But it's only one god.
And so therefore, I don't have any problems feeling very comfortable going into church, and saying my prayers that I know, or going to a synagogue and still saying my prayers that I know.
So it doesn't matter to me.
Because I feel very comfortable in any place.
I guess in a way, much more so than both of my parents, because my father was-- until he died, he was-- he was cremated.
He-- that's what he wanted.
He was not-- I mean he really-- his hatred to religion-- religion was incredible.
It was permeating all-- all over.
And that's why I've never learned any customs, anything.
Because they didn't exist in our home.
My mother, the same thing.
She's not religious.
She's not-- she doesn't hate religion.
But she's not religious.
She's-- when I say to her, Mom, it would be a good idea for you, for example, since she lives in Century Village in-- in Florida.
I said, why don't you join the synagogue?
You know, you meet some nice people, whatever.
It will be nice for you.
And so she looks at me and she says, what do you expect me to do?
All of a sudden now I should go enjoy synagogue?
I never belonged to one, you know, when I was younger.
Never in my life, I was always sort of Bundist, socialist, or whatever.
Now you're telling me to go and become a religious person?
But I don't feel that way.
To me, it's just-- I-- I really-- I like churches.
I like synagogues.
And I don't feel that this is any inconsistency.
And I am married to a Protestant.
And there I'm laughing again.
Because my father said it at one time to me, I mean jokingly he said, don't marry a religious Jew.
Don't marry a religious Roman Catholic.
Just marry a Protestant.
And I didn't marry a Protestant because my father told me, but it just happened I fell in love with a Protestant.
You know, so it's-- it's really-- it's-- it's funny.
And he feels about religion basically the same way as I do.
We-- we got married at the United Nations Plaza, you know.
It-- that's anybody can get married there, Muslim, Jew, Christian, Buddhist, whoever.
And that's what I like to see, and I would like to see more of it that people feel, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] you know, love for-- for one another, and whatever you are.
And that's basically my feelings about religion.
Yes.
I-- thought about it.
And I think that no matter what, you know, I still-- you know, I still would be a person who is very open-minded about things.
Because I think this is within you, and I think that education always meant a lot to me.
So no matter who I would be with, I still would go to school.
Because I enjoyed education very much.
But the fact-- being with my parents, especially with my father, who was extremely-- he was a self-educated man, basically.
But he was reading non-stop.
He was like the walking encyclopedia.
If I-- if I needed to look up something, let's say, what's happening in the history.
What happened during, you know, Hannibal or something like that?
Or what happened in-- in Egypt, whatever, or what happened in ancient some history times?
I would go first to him.
He would talk to me about it, give me a whole lecture.
And then he would send me to look it up in an encyclopedia, you know, or any books.
Go to the library and check it out.
And I would come back, and I said, you told me the whole thing.
So why do you send me, you know, to just look it up again?
And-- so the-- the love for knowledge was always there, but within me as well.
Do you think you would have retained your anti-Jewish attitudes?
I doubt it.
I doubt it very much.
But you-- you never know.
I probably would be one of-- one of those Poles who, I know quite a few of them.
And that's why I-- I can't tolerate the idea when people tell you all Poles are antisemites, all Poles are horrible.
Because that's not the case.
There are many antisemites, true.
But there are some wonderful people too.
You know, so I just don't believe that because of that I shouldn't go to visit Poland.
I mean, I was told by several of my mother friends-- my mother's friends, aren't you embarrassed even to go, you know, to Poland, to this horrible place, you know, where so many Jews were killed?
And I said but if not some of these people, many Jews would not be alive.
I wouldn't be alive here, if not these-- if not these Roman Catholic people.
And I know very, very many people who were very helpful to Jews.
You know, I-- I have right here, I have a lady upstairs who was the same way as my mother was, in Warsaw ghetto, with me.
She was, you know, living in Warsaw ghetto.
And she was smuggled out by Roman Catholics.
She was in their home.
Just before leaving, before they found her another space, when the entire family was wiped out.
Because they were helping Jews.
And she just luckily, you know, she said to them, I feel uncomfortable being here.
So get me out of here.
And the same night, SS came and destroyed the entire family.
So I mean we can't say that every Pole was like that.
So there were some good ones as well.
And I just hope that Jews won't be so against the Roman Catholics and vice versa.
Probably the fact, you know, if you go and you educate yourself, and you read, and-- and you meet Jews, and you meet other people from other countries, you become open-minded about it.
And I think definitely that would happen.
Because I would have gone to school.
So I would have seen something different.
I just-- I can't believe that I-- you know, that I would be some kind of raised as just arch destructor of Jews or something like that.
I just-- I can't believe in that.
I really cannot.
I think that the-- I think that the entire-- I think that this is unusual.
I don't think that every priest was like that, and I don't think-- I think that also the Roman Catholicism is changing.
And it's-- it's changing because there is, you know, the pope is different.
And it always has an idea of who is up at the helm, and what kind of things they teach you, you know.
So I think it has to do with politics, in general, world politics.
And what was incredible, I recall, when we were still in Poland in 1956, and when the Jews were fighting the Arabs, yes?
That was the first war.
We're still there.
And how proud the Poles were that the Jews were actually winning.
That they would say our [NON-ENGLISH], meaning, you know, our Jews.
It's not-- it's like diminutive.
You know, it's like it's so funny.
It's so funny.
I mean, I just I-- I-- I couldn't believe it at first, to hear this.
But they were so really proud that these are the Jews that were from Poland, or were from close by that they were their Jews who actually won, you know, over the Arabs.
I mean, it's just-- it's crazy.
It's that the whole-- the whole idea, the whole notion is just beyond belief.
But they were so proud of their Jews, you know.
It's-- it's very, very difficult to understand.
But-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] what-- what can you say about it?
I mean it's just it's-- such it's almost craziness, you know?
I just hope that there is no prejudice.
And I really genuinely hope that the prejudice should not come from either side.
And I hope that Polish Roman Catholic people will have a better understanding of Jews, and-- and vice versa.
And the Jews will understand Poles better.
Because to some degree, I believe that why-- why do we have such open society here in the United States?
Jews don't live in ghettos.
They don't isolate themselves.
And I know that a lot of it was historically because Jews could not live anyplace they wanted to, or Jews could not buy land.
I understand all that.
But to some degree, it is incredible to me that Jews would be living from 1430, like my father's documents show, which I have-- and they would not learn the language properly.
I mean, it's-- it's beyond belief.
But a lot of Jews spoke Polish so poorly that you could not believe it.
They basically were totally-- they were isolating themselves in a lot of ways.
And I think that fostered the prejudices as well.
And I am against that.
I hope the society is open both ways.
You're quite welcome.
And thank you for letting me say what I had to say.
